focus: Resolve seam continuity with minimal edits.
next_head_excerpt:
# Chapter 17

The paper visitor badge kept peeling at one corner under Marisol's thumb. The county hospital had tried to smell clean for visiting hours and failed in a more expensive way than the train ever did. In the orthopedic waiting bay, lemon disinfectant sat on top of fryer oil, old mop water, and the wool dampness of people who had slept in chairs. The television above the vending machines mouthed silent weather over closed captions that lagged half a sentence behind. Someone had set the air-conditioning too low. Her damp shirt cooled against her spine. The release papers from the municipal holding room were folded in her bag with the parking stub and the public defender's card, all of it softening at the edges from her hands.

Fallout had arranged itself into rows of molded plastic chairs.

A young woman from one of the family bunks sat under a county shelter blanket with a blue cast running from knuckles to elbow, her broken wrist balanced on a diaper bag printed with cartoon lemons. Her little boy slept across two chairs with his face pressed into the vinyl, mouth open, one sneaker untied. Across from them a Caldera laundry woman Marisol knew by sight, though not by name, held a slinged arm against her chest and argued into her phone in a voice gone flat from repetition. No, she told whoever was not helping her, she had clocked in before the platform stop. Yes, the supervisor had seen her. No, she did not have another copy of the incident form because they had taken the first one with the bloody shirt. Farther down the row a custody officer with a scratch through his eyebrow sat with his thumb splinted and his knees spread in the defeated posture of a man who had expected his badge to exempt him from pain.

At the desk, the unit clerk wore a tiny silver thimb...
prev_tail_excerpt:
...he scraps on it were not enough to break the corridor. They had only dirtied the white gloves. Soon, when the train reached the right piece of track and all the compromised people kept their nerve, the same system would carry more than scraps.

For now she did what Teresa needed done. She pulled the ruined towels, reset the service cart, lifted the fallen rosemary pot, and turned the closed lavatory sign inward so the camera would not catch it on the next pass. Sabotage was not always an explosion. Sometimes it was knowing exactly how much function to restore so power would walk farther out onto a rotten beam.

The overhead announcement chimed in its clipped synthetic politeness. Public station approach. Prepare for demonstration stop. Remain in assigned area.

In the observation car, the investors sat straighter. The photographer checked his batteries. Deputy Minister Baeza asked quietly for water and was given it in a paper cup stamped with the RailPassage seal above a line of tiny legal text about indemnity. Sena tucked the corrupted menu deeper into her notebook and looked, for the first time all morning, less entertained than hungry.

Marisol unclipped the supervisor passcard from her chest and stood by the sealed door, listening to the train carry its separate truths in the same frame: rosemary and blackwater, linen and restraint hardware, Teresa's polished vowels and the cough from the hidden car behind them. The station roofs were coming into view now, white in the noon glare, and the sewage note was beginning to outlast the citrus. Nobody on board could pretend not to notice.

That was enough. The run was wobbling, and because too many important people had already promised themselves a clean story, they were going to carry the wobble straight into public sight.
